item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Came back from a small hiatus and wasn't sure about this as my first SCP after not reading for a while. I already want more. Have another +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Experiment proposal: Place device hosting SCP-3137 on top of SCP-1875. Leave alone for a while.
See what this pair of sweet gals comes up with when conferring with that other pair of sweet gals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, the main anomaly is basically a Thing What Lets You Do Things, but between "They are already contained" and that final note (for all that it's kind of goofy in places), the purpose behind this becomes known and it's just creepy as heck. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how the final note starts off sounding like a massive threat, switches to an ad, then back into a threat again.

item.title: Staff post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Troll nonsense, please ignore.

Article has been reverted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait a min, is it about computer system or sex slaves

item.title: This is what happens when FCC gets control of the night
item.content:encoded: Pay to unlock it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
if any words are typed in English

what if they're non-English words that have the exact same spelling as English words but totally different meanings?

pain, main, gift, mist, etc

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed everything up until the final note, but then didn't really enjoy that at all. The whole "here's why we did what we did" thing never sits well with me. I think I'm at a novote despite my gross giggling at the baby don't hurt me command.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat concept that doesn't overstay its welcome and sits nicely on the line between funny and terrifying. The only thing I'd like to change is for that final note to be cut down a bit, both in length and tone, to something more like a winzip pop-up blackmailing you with eternal day, but I suppose that's more of a tonal preference for me then an actual criticism.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh wow ok. That was awesome, I was a bit unsure at the start, but at the end you really made up for it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was perfect. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the perfect example of how to use a journal entry to drive home the point of an SCP. Absolutely perfect. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was very cute until it was very, very not.

Gotta love them old gods. I wonder what the conversion rate is on human sacrifice to USD?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It really breaks immersion to try and imagine why on Earth they'd be testing something so inherently unpredictable with cutesy commands seemingly written for kicks. Like, humor and inanity is fine, but for me it's got to have some basis in reality. Here, it feels like it's just trying to force punny cleverness, and it's really not at all satisfying to me.

Lost interest before I could make it through till the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This neatly avoids a lot of the computer skip problems, so points for that.

There's a few tone issues here and there, but overall good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love the theme of commercialization of authenticity at the end. Great skip, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The testing log is great, and the ending is creepy. That being said, this reminds me a little of spikebrennan's idea sex machine. However, I still upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Obviously, the command to disappear the babies is "no more"

Anyways, the last part seems heavily inspired in the Rick and morty Citadel's Waffers (which yeah, it's a plus for me because I'm that cheap) with some pretty tasty spookyness. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like having a collaborative log would detract a little from the spookiness of this mysterious application and its creators. In my opinion, a lot of SCPs with large collaborative logs are known more for the log's contents than the SCP itself. For example, 914 turns things into other things, but the focus is on the series of inputs/outputs rather than the machine itself. Maybe having a log would turn the focus of this SCP from the notepad and its creators, to simply a list of "this is what we typed and this is what happened". Maybe it'll all work out though. This is all just my ramblings on the subject, make of it what you will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I was thinking of adding a collaborative log at some point and letting people come up with ideas for that themselves, if this succeeds :p.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, though I feel you're kind of missing a big question here by not dealing with what happens when entire sentences are typed in, or huge blocks of text are pasted in before you hit return.
I originally thought the note at the end was going to ruin the whole thing, but that actually turned out well too. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what is love

Huh, it summons babies that attack people? Weird. Let's see where this goes

baby don't hurt me

Oh god fucking dammit. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here comes the night!
Here comes the night!
Look out, here comes the night!

With thanks to the following for their critique:

NineVolt (all credit for giving me the idea for Nitepad to actually do something malicious towards the Foundation)
Garckbloom
Folding Papers
moklin
Modern_Erasmus
taylor_itkin

This skip was inspired by a typo I made when trying to find notepad :p.

You can find more of my stuff here.
